title: What is Backpropagation?
concept: Backpropagation is the backward pass that starts from the loss score and traces how each weight contributed to the error so the model knows what to change.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the first thing the network has after finishing the forward pass?

options.text: A prediction and a loss score measuring how wrong it was.

exercises.explain: Correct! The forward pass ends with a prediction, and the loss score measures the size of the error.

cards.title: Who messed up the group project?
cards.body: Imagine a class group project earns a low score. The teacher does not just say, "Bad job" and walk away. They look backward through the slides, research, and speaking parts to see which choices caused the weak result. Backpropagation does this for a neural network.

cards.title: The backward pass
cards.body: Backpropagation is the learning phase that runs after the loss is calculated. It sends the error signal backward through the network and measures how much each weight helped cause the mistake. Those measurements are called gradients.

cards.title: Watching the replay in reverse
cards.body: A coach studies a replay after a team loses. They rewind the video from the final goal back through each pass to find the earlier mistakes that set it up. Backpropagation rewinds the model from loss back to the earlier weights.

cards.title: Tracing a wrong animal guess
cards.body: A model sees a cat photo but predicts "dog" with high confidence, so the loss is large. Backpropagation starts at that loss and sends feedback backward: the output weights get blamed first, then the hidden-layer weights that shaped the wrong pattern also receive part of the blame.

cards.title: Weights do not travel backward
cards.body: It can sound like the actual numbers in the model are moving backward through the layers. They are not. What travels backward is a correction signal that tells each weight whether it pushed the prediction in a helpful or harmful direction.

cards.title: Forward pass vs. Backpropagation
cards.body: The two halves of one training step:

left.text:
Inputs move toward the output.
Model makes a prediction.
Loss measures the error.

right.text:
Starts at the loss.
Traces blame backward.
Computes gradients for weights.

exercises.prompt: What is the main job of backpropagation?

options.text: Figure out how much each weight contributed to the loss so the model can be corrected.
Shuffle the dataset before training starts.
Delete the output layer after each mistake.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Backpropagation is about assigning credit or blame to weights based on the final loss.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Backpropagation begins at the loss node and moves backward through the network.
exercises.explain: True! The error score at the loss node is the starting point for the backward pass.

exercises.sentence: Backpropagation helps the model learn by tracing the error back to each ___.
exercises.explain: Correct. The goal is to measure how each weight influenced the final mistake.

exercises.prompt: Which description sounds like backpropagation in action?

options.text: A network gets a high loss, then sends that error backward to compute how each layer should adjust.
A network guesses a label and immediately stops training forever.
A network hides the loss score so the weights never see it.

exercises.explain: Correct! Backpropagation is the backward flow of error information used to compute corrections.

exercises.prompt: A handwriting model predicts the digit "8" when the true label is "3," so the loss becomes large. What does backpropagation do next?

options.text: It uses the loss to send a correction signal backward and estimate how each weight contributed to the wrong guess.
It deletes the example because the model already failed once.
It changes the true label from "3" to "8" so the loss becomes zero.
It skips directly to testing on new data.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Backpropagation turns the loss into layer-by-layer feedback, telling the network which weights helped produce the wrong answer. Next, we will unpack the core idea that makes this tracing possible: the chain rule.
